critic_raw:
This is a well-structured experiment design with several genuinely careful choices: preprocessing fit on training only, equivalent tuning budgets, Brier score as primary metric, bootstrap CIs, and explicit ablations. However, I find two genuine methodological flaws and one important prerequisite gap that together could invalidate the core comparison.

**Issue 1: Temporal leakage via random repository-stratified split**

The specific claim: Stratifying by repository identity and defect label while ignoring temporal ordering of PRs produces a valid train/test split.

Why it's wrong: PRs from the same repository are not exchangeable across time. Repository state evolves — team membership, coding standards, CI infrastructure, dependency stacks, defect rates all shift. A random 60/20/20 split will, with high probability, intermix early and late PRs across folds. This creates several leakage vectors: (a) the 'author historical defect rate' feature is computed on training data — but if a test-set PR from month 30 is 'trained against' PRs from month 32 of the same author, the historical rate includes future information; (b) more subtly, code churn patterns, file-change correlations, and reviewer pairings carry temporal autocorrelation, so the model can use patterns that effectively 'know' about future repository state. The transformer in particular, being a large-capacity model with text features that can memorize repository-specific language patterns, will exploit this more than the baseline. This would inflate the transformer's advantage on the test set relative to actual deployment performance. What would constitute evidence: Compare split strategies — random stratified vs. a strict temporal holdout (train on months 1–22, validate 23–26, test 27–36) — and measure whether the performance gap between transformer and baseline shrinks under the temporal split. If the gap is materially smaller under temporal splitting, temporal leakage is the cause. If it persists, the split choice is not the driver.

**Issue 2: Author historical defect rate is an asymmetric feature**

The specific claim: Author historical defect rate, computed on training data only for both models, is a fair feature.

Why it's wrong: The baseline is explicitly designed around hand-engineered features and uses this rate directly. The transformer, however, also receives PR title, description, and review comments as text — and author identity or authorship signals may be encoded implicitly in writing style, vocabulary, or reviewer names appearing in text. The transformer can thus 'rediscover' author history from text in ways the baseline cannot, even if author ID is not explicitly provided. This is not an ablation-controlled asymmetry: the ablation proposed (no text features) would remove this entirely, but nothing tests the contribution of author-correlated text signals specifically. The practical consequence is that the transformer may appear to understand code risk when it is actually exploiting author-identity proxies in prose. What would constitute evidence: Anonymize all author-identifying text fields (replace author names, reviewer names, and @-mentions with tokens) and re-run the comparison. If the performance gap narrows significantly, the transformer's advantage is author-identity leakage from text, not code understanding. A further test: measure whether the transformer's predicted defect rate has high mutual information with author identity after controlling for PR size.

**Issue 3: Labeling window creates a systematic survivorship asymmetry between repositories (prerequisite gap)**

The specific claim: A 7–14 day post-merge window for defect labeling is uniformly applicable across all 10–20 repositories.

Why it's wrong: Enterprise repositories vary enormously in release cadence. A team releasing weekly will surface defects within the window routinely; a team releasing quarterly will not have user-facing defect reports within 14 days of merge even for genuinely defective PRs. This means the label noise rate is repository-dependent and correlated with repository identity — one of the primary stratification variables. The macro-averaged Brier score will then reflect not only model quality but differential label noise across repositories, and a model that learns 'this is a slow-release repo, predict low defect rate' will score well without learning anything about code quality. The 85% manual agreement check on ~500 PRs is insufficient to detect this if the sample is drawn uniformly across repositories, because slow-release repos may have few positive labels in any 500-PR sample. What would constitute evidence: Report per-repository positive label rate alongside per-repository release cadence or deployment frequency. If positive label rate is strongly correlated with release cadence (faster teams have higher labeling density), the labeling mechanism is confounded. A robustness check would be to restrict the comparison to repositories with similar release cadences and see whether the transformer advantage is stable.

**Summary**: Issue 1 (temporal leakage) is the most serious because it asymmetrically benefits the higher-capacity model and would cause the experiment to over-estimate the transformer's real-world advantage. Issue 2 (author identity in text) is a confound that could misattribute the source of any genuine gain. Issue 3 (label window vs. release cadence) undermines the validity of the macro-averaged primary metric. None of these are implementation nits — they are structural choices that could produce a plausible-looking transformer win that does not reflect actual code-understanding capability.
all_issues_raised: Random repository-stratified split ignores temporal ordering, creating leakage that asymmetrically benefits the higher-capacity transformer over the baseline.
Author historical defect rate can be implicitly re-encoded by the transformer from prose text, creating an asymmetric feature advantage not controlled by the proposed ablation.
The 7–14 day defect labeling window produces repository-dependent label noise correlated with release cadence, confounding the macro-averaged Brier score primary metric.
all_issues_adjudicated: Random repository-stratified split ignores temporal ordering, creating leakage that asymmetrically benefits the higher-capacity transformer over the baseline.
Author historical defect rate can be implicitly re-encoded by the transformer from prose text, creating an asymmetric feature advantage not controlled by the proposed ablation.
The 7–14 day defect labeling window produces repository-dependent label noise correlated with release cadence, confounding the macro-averaged Brier score primary metric.
